coredata.dc:description: Exploring a facile, low-cost, scalable, and environmentally friendly fluorescent superhydrophobic material is urgent and significant for the further development of superhydrophobic materials. In this work, we developed two series of superhydrophobic materials with different scale hierarchical structures. 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT possessed a dual-scale hierarchical structure and showed a WCA of 155.62 ± 1° and a WSA of 4.8 ± 0.2°. On this basis, by introducing CDs, the denser multi-scale hierarchical structure of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT was achieved resulting in a higher WCA of 169.22 ± 2° and a lower WSA of 2.3 ± 0.2°. FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT possessed a fluorescence property derived from the doping of biomass carbon quantum dots prepared from banana peels and displayed an emission band located at 430 nm. In addition, FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT also exhibited good heat and dispersion stability. Interestingly, chemical resistance was significantly improved after introducing CDs. It is anticipated that this work will provide a facile and general approach with the industrial potential to fabricate superhydrophobic materials with hierarchical micro-nano structures and blue fluorescence for self-cleaning and light conversion applications.

head.ce:title: Combination of carbon dots for the design of superhydrophobic fluorescent materials with bioinspired micro-nano multiscale hierarchical structure

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring a facile, low-cost, scalable, and environmentally friendly fluorescent superhydrophobic material is urgent and significant for the further development of superhydrophobic materials. In this work, we developed two series of superhydrophobic materials with different scale hierarchical structures. 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT possessed a dual-scale hierarchical structure and showed a WCA of 155.62 ± 1° and a WSA of 4.8 ± 0.2°. On this basis, by introducing CDs, the denser multi-scale hierarchical structure of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT was achieved resulting in a higher WCA of 169.22 ± 2° and a lower WSA of 2.3 ± 0.2°. FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT possessed a fluorescence property derived from the doping of biomass carbon quantum dots prepared from banana peels and displayed an emission band located at 430 nm. In addition, FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT also exhibited good heat and dispersion stability. Interestingly, chemical resistance was significantly improved after introducing CDs. It is anticipated that this work will provide a facile and general approach with the industrial potential to fabricate superhydrophobic materials with hierarchical micro-nano structures and blue fluorescence for self-cleaning and light conversion applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the Wenzel or Cassie‐Baxter models, rough surfaces feature enhanced wetting properties [1–4]. The solid surface exhibits consistent superhydrophobicity but has a high and low adhesion capacity for liquid droplets, mainly due to the difference in the corresponding size and roughness of the surface microstructure. Researchers concluded that the surface of the lotus leaf had papillae of ∼10 µm in size with an average spacing of ∼20 µm [5,6]. The magnification of the papillae 10,000 times revealed a dense distribution of rod-like wax structures of ∼1 µm in length and ∼100 nm in diameter on the surface of the papillae [7,8]. Same nanoscale rod-like wax structures were also present in the grooves between the papillae [9]. This composite multi-scale micro-nano structure can trap the nanoscale air layers, allowing water droplets to fall only on the top of the papillary epidermal cells and thus roll easily, thereby carrying away dust particles [10,11]. However, when size of this dual-scale micro-nano structure becomes larger or looser, water droplets will enter larger grooves, resulting in a higher adhesion force on the surface [12]. It can be seen that different scales and densities of the composite micro-nano structures on solid surfaces will present different hydrophobic properties [13]. Obviously, hydrophobic dense microscopic gaps will be a critical factor for constructing a superhydrophobic surface with the “Lotus Effect”. Building micro-nano structures with suitable layer distribution and arrangement is conducive to the formation of the air layers.

In recent years, superhydrophobic materials have been extensively developed by etching, electrostatic spinning, chemical vapor deposition, and self-assembly as various nano-fillers added to polymers for self-cleaning, anti-icing, anti-fogging, and resistance to biological contaminants [12,14–17]. However, there are only few reports of superhydrophobic materials with fluorescent properties, which limits the application of superhydrophobic materials in agriculture, solar panels, fluorescent imaging, etc. Recently, Zhang et al. prepared hybrid silica spheres using 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) and obtained hydrophobic nanoparticles with blue and yellow fluorescence after high-temperature calcination and modification [18]. You et al. obtained superhydrophobic materials with bright fluorescence by encapsulating chalcogenide quantum dots in superhydrophobic silica aerogels [19]. Wang et al. prepared a novel kind of TEMPO-oxidized cellulose microfibril-based luminescent paper by using oxidized cellulose as a deposition template, depositing rare-earth ions on the surface, and forming lanthanide-based nanocrystal/nanotitania-doped cellulose fibers [20]. Although the involvement of rare earth, fluorescent dyes and conjugated polymers have made some progress in these systems [21–25], it still restricts the sustainable development of multifunctional fluorescent superhydrophobic materials. In addition, many approaches for constructing hierarchical micro-nano structures such as self-assembly [26–28], etching and electrochemical deposition [29,30] are substrate-dependent and require complex process conditions or specialized apparatuses, which are constrained to scalable preparation. Therefore, there is still a strong need to explore an alternative and reliable strategy to produce superhydrophobic fluorescent materials with dense micro-nano structure, along with the ability to easily scale up in practice.

Carbon dots (CDs), as new carbon nanoparticles, are widely used in bioimaging [31,32], catalysis [33,34], and drug delivery [35–37] due to their excellent fluorescence properties. Compared with fluorescent organic dyes, rare earth nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots prepared from biomass ensure higher biosafety, lower cost and green chemistry [38,39]. However, despite some success in the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots in solution, the fluorescence quenching during compounding and curing with other materials are significant. Therefore, the fabrication of CDs composites that maintain high and stable luminescence in the solid-state remains challenging. As a natural and cheap material, Montmorillonite (MMT) may be a good choice to build micron scale roughness due to its micron-sized lamellar structure. However, MMT is hydrophilic and needs to be modified to a more hydrophobic state.

Based on the above consideration, in this work, we designed two series of superhydrophobic nanocomposites with different scale hierarchical structure (dual-scale and multi-scale) in a an inexpensive broadly applicable method. Among them, the materials with multi-scale hierarchical structure exhibited fluorescent properties due to the introduction of CDs. And it was the first time to fabricated fluorescent superhydrophobic nanocomposites by combining with clay and CDs. Specifically, as for dual-scale hierarchical structure, the natural micron scale of montmorillonite (MMT) is used to provide micron-scale structure in the “lotus leaf” effect and used to integrate roughness in micro-nanoscale. In situ hydrolytic polymerization of ethyl orthosilicate on the MMT layers surface was used to generate silica spheres to obtain nanoscale structure of intermediate size and PFOTES was used to reduce the surface energy of the materials ( Scheme 1). On this basis, small-sized nanoscale carbon quantum dots were added during the synthesis of multi-scale hierarchical structure to make them uniformly dispersed on the surface of micron-sized MMT and medium-sized SiO2 nanospheres. At the same time, it constituted a multiscale micro-nano structure with micron-size, medium-nano size and small-nano size so as to obtain a denser microscopic gap. Multi-scale hierarchical structure materials showed better superhydrophobicity, dispersion stability and chemical resistance in testing. This work provides a feasible method for the large-scale preparation of superhydrophobic fluorescent materials. Thus, FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT that served as superhydrophobic fluorescent fillers possesses great potential in the fabrication of self-cleaning, light conversion films or coatings, which will be investigated in our subsequent work.

TEOS (3.40 g), KH-570 (0.27 g), IPA (7.10 g), MMT (9.26 g，6.95 g and 5.56 g) were added into a three-necked flask, adjusted pH to 4 by hydrochloric acid (HCl), and stirred for 15 min at 60 °C. Then half amount of distilled water (0.65 g) was slowly added followed by the addition of 2.50 g PFOTES, then added the other half amount of distilled water, and the mixture was refluxed for 13 h. Finally, the mixed solution was ground and sifted after dried in a drying oven at 50 ℃ to get the powder form, which is designated as 0.3FSi-SiO2@MMT, 0.4FSi-SiO2@MMT and 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT.

MMT (3 g) was added to 120 mL of deionized water and stirred for 30 min at room temperature, then added NaOH (0.1 mol/L) to adjust the pH to 12. the reaction was conducted by water bath at 80 °C and magnetic stirring for 3 h. Then 3 CEC of STAB was added to 50 mL of deionized water and stirred to dissolve followed by mixing with the above mixture and continuing stirring for 3 h. The final product was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and ethanol three times. After drying, OMMT was obtained.

In short, Banana peels (1 g) and citric acid (1 g) were added into 20 mL of deionized water and stirred until the citric acid was dissolved at room temperature, then added ammonia to adjust the pH to 7. After that, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling, the obtained product was filtered using a Millipore filter (0.22 µm) to remove the impurities and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove residual solvents and then purified by dialysis (molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da) for 72 h. After freeze-dried, the pure CDs were obtained, then stored at 4 ℃ for further use.
CDs with the mass of 0.1, 0.02 and 0.01 of OMMT were dissolved in 2.5 mL of deionized water and ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min to obtain CDs solutions with different concentrations.

The MMT and deionized water in 2.1 was substituted by 5.56 g OMMT 1.25 mL of CDs solution, and the multi-scale hierarchical structure materials were obtained after drying and grounding, which were marked as FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT、FSi-SiO2/0.02CDs@OMMT and FSi-SiO2/0.01CDs@OMMT according to the CDs content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of FSi-SiO2/@MMT and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of superhydrophobic materials with different scale hierarchical structures was revealed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images, as shown in Fig. 1. and Fig. 2. MMT presented a lamellar structure with a smooth and clear surface, and the layers were stacked together (Fig. 1a, e). The inset TEM images in Fig. 1e also reveals the stacked MMT layers with an average size of 1 µm (the dark black part). After the in-situ hydrolysis of TEOS on the surface of MMT lamellae to form nanostructured SiO2 particles (average size ∼46.7 nm) and the grafting of KH-570 and PFOTES, the modified MMT surface became rough. It was the micron-level structure of MMT and the nanostructure of SiO2 nanoparticles evenly dispersed on its surface that together constitute the dual-scale hierarchical structure of FSi-SiO2@MMT, which was like the “rose petal” structure (Fig. 1.b, f). For FSi-SiO2@MMT, the MMT surface became rougher when the FSi-SiO2 content increased to 0.4 and 0.5. The edges of MMT lamellae tended to be curly in this process and the degree of curling reached the maximum at the FSi-SiO2 content of 0.5 (Fig. 1c, g, d, h), showing a more obvious double scale hierarchical structure with an average size of 1274 nm (Fig. S1). KH-570 and PFOTES wrapped around SiO2 could be seen in the inset images of Fig. 1h, showing brighter colors because they could not be absorbed by the dye solution. TEM morphologies and HRTEM of CDs were shown in Fig. S2. It could be observed in Fig. S2a that CDs displayed a globoid with a size of ∼5 nm (the red circle part). The HRTEM image (Fig. S2b) shows that CDs have good crystallinity and form a continuous crystal phase with distinct lattice stripe spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the lattice stripe of the d001 crystal plane in a typical graphite structure. As for FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT, in SEM images (Fig. 2a, e), OMMT (average size ∼0.89 µm) presented a curlier lamellar structure compared with MMT, and an irregular horizontal and vertical arrangement of the lamellas. The flat lamellar structure of OMMT decreased with increasing content of CDs and was replaced by increasing agglomerated "rock-like" micropapillary structures. (Fig. 2b, c, d, f, g, h). Among them, micron structures were provided by OMMT layers (average size ∼1 µm), medium-sized nanostructure were provided by SiO2 nanoparticles (average size ∼30 nm), and smaller nanostructure were obtained from CDs (average size ∼5 nm), which together formed a tightly multiscale hierarchical structure that were similar to the "lotus leaf " surface structure.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were performed to compare the chemical compositions and structure of two superhydrophobic materials with different scale hierarchical structures. In Fig. 3a, both MMT and FSi-SiO2@MMT showed a very strong absorption band at 1090 cm−1 assigned to the stretching modes of the Si-O-Si groups, as well as 3628 cm−1 peaks assigned to the stretching modes of the O-H groups, 3456 cm−1 and 1642 cm−1 peaks attributed to the stretching and flexural vibration of H-O-H groups. In addition, the intensity of these peaks decreased with the decreasing addition of MMT, this could be explained by the more intense hydrolysis reaction between the O-H groups on MMT and other modifiers (Fig. 3a) [40,41]. Compared with MMT, many new characteristic peaks could be observed in the series of FSi-SiO2@MMT. The absorption peaks at 2978–2899 cm−1 were attributed to -CH3 and -CH2 [42]. The absorption peaks of -CF2, -CF3 and C-F stretching modes were observed at 1205–1440 cm−1 and 680–785 cm−1, demonstrating the existence of fluorine grafted onto the surface of MMT (Fig. 3a). For FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT (Fig. 3c), in addition to the characteristic peaks of OMMT, the new characteristic peaks at 1400 cm−1, 1580 cm−1 and 1674 cm−1 assigned to C-N, -COO- and CO function groups were mainly originated from the CDs (Fig. S3) [21,43–47]. Moreover, these peaks showed a slight shift in peak position, proving the successful preparation of the FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT series. XRD patterns of MMT, 0.3FSi-SiO2 @MMT, 0.4FSi-SiO2 @MMT and 0.5FSi-SiO2 @MMT were given in Fig. 3b. The layer spacing was calculated to be 1.525 nm, 1.496 nm, 1.489 nm and 1.554 nm respectively according to the Bragg Eq. (1):
(1)

2
,
3 ; λ ≈ 0.154 nm )

The layer spacing of modified MMT decreased first and then increased with the decrease of the additional amount. A reasonable explanation was that as the proportion of TEOS, PFOTES and KH-570 increased, the surface grafting sites of MMT tended to be saturated, and the connection mode between MMT and modifiers also changed from surface grafting to a small amount of intercalation, thus expanding the interlayer distance[48–50]. In other words, if there were enough hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of MMT to co-polycondensated with modifiers, a large number of functional groups of modifiers would be grafted on the surface of MMT first, and the steric hindrance effect would slightly compress the layer spacing of MMT, resulting in the reduction of layer spacing of 0.3FSi-SiO2@MMT and 0.4FSi-SiO2@MMT [51,52]. However, as the addition of MMT decreased and there was no surplus of active functional groups on the surface, the small molecular weight of unhydrolyzed TEOS, PFOTES and KH-570 would enter the interlayer of the MMT, thus increasing the layer spacing by 0.0295 nm. In addition, it was observed that there was no change in other crystal planes of MMT and the FSi-SiO2 @MMT, indicating that the modified MMT still retains the original basic crystal form and structure. In Fig. 3d, after modification, the layer spacing of OMMT was significantly expanded to 2.43 nm (2θ = 3.63°). As for FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT series, the d001 peaks occurred at 4.83°, 4.61° and 4.24°, corresponding to the layer spacings of 1.83 nm, 1.91 nm and 2.08 nm respectively. A broadening of the primary diffraction peaks (001) and a decrease in the intensity of the peaks could be observed in these XRD curves, indicating a more dispersed lamellar structure in FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT series. This was due to the small size of the CDs, which could be partially inserted into the interlayer of montmorillonite following the plenty of organic chains of small molecules, so as to further expanded the lamella of OMMT, and to some extent reduced the lamellar crystalline integrity of OMMT.

Further in-depth detection of the functional groups of MMT, 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT were carried out through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. Compared with MMT, it was indicated in the survey spectrum ( Fig. 4a) that 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT not only contained the characteristic peaks of Si2s and O1s but also had the peaks of C1s (290.0 eV) and F1s (683.0 eV). The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 4b) contains six different types of carbons: C-Si (284.0 eV), C-C/CC (285.1 eV), C-O (286.5 eV), CO (288.4 eV), C-F(CH2) (290.8 eV) and C-F(CF3) (293.2 eV) [53,54]. The high-resolution F1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) reveals two different types of fluorine: the peaks at 682.3 and 683.8 eV were attributed to C-F(CF2) and C-F(CF3) [55,56]. Moreover, the high-resolution Si2P spectrum (Fig. 4d) was resolved into two peaks located at the binding energies of 97.3 eV and 96.2 eV, which could be assigned to Si-C, Si-O-Si, indicating the MMT had been successfully modified with SiO2, KH-570 and PFOTES. Separately, besides the characteristic peak of C, F, Si, and O elements ( Fig. 5a-d, f), the characteristic peaks of trace N elements also appeared in XPS spectra of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT. The N 1 s XPS image contains three fitting peaks at 400.20 eV, 403.08 eV, and 420.36 eV (Fig. 5e), which can be ascribed to the C-N-C, C-N-H, and (C)3-N bonds of STAB and the -NH2 of CDs. It proved the success of the initial modification of MMT by STAB and the secondary modification by TEOS, KH-570, PFOTES and CDs. The O 1 s XPS image (Fig. 5f) could be fitted into five binding energies at 531.51, 532.10, 532.66, 533.17, and 533.55 eV, arising from CC, O-Al-O, O-Si-O, C-OH/C-O-C and OC-OH bonds, respectively, which was highly consistent with FT-IR spectrum.

The element mapping analyses were carried out in a random area of 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT. As for 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT, in Fig. S4a, Al and Ca were mainly from MMT, while C and F were mainly from KH-570 and PFOTES, O and Si existed after modification partly from SiO2 nanoparticles, which further proved that KH-570, PFOTES and SiO2 nanoparticles were successfully grafted on the surface of MMT. Moreover, the EDX results were consistent with element mapping. As shown in Fig. S4b, the O element occupied the most, followed by Si, C, Al and F elements respectively, which was consistent with element mapping, further indicating the 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT were successfully prepared. New N elements from STAB and CDs occurred in the elemental mapping of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT (Fig. S5a) compared with 0.5FSi-SiO2 @MMT, proving that the CDs were successfully grafted on the surface of OMMT, and STAB was successfully inserted into the interlayer of OMMT. The same results were also proved in the EDX spectrum of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT (Fig. S5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical properties of CDs and FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT
ce:section.ce:para:
To thoroughly investigate the luminescent properties of the CDs, a series of optical characterizations were performed. CDs showed two apparent absorption peaks at 235 and 332 nm in the UV-Vis absorption spectra ( Fig. 6a), corresponding to the π-π * transition of the aromatic CC (SP2) bond and n-π * transition of the CO/CN bonds in carbon cores [57,58]. As shown in the inset illustration in Fig. 6a, CDs solution showed transparent yellowish-brown in daylight and turned bright blue in ultraviolet light. It can be seen in Fig. S6a, CDs held the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 320 and 433 nm respectively. Then, PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths were measured and a slightly excitation-dependent feature was observed (Fig. 6b). The PL intensity increased with the excitation wavelength changed from 260 nm to 320 nm, and then reduced as the excitation wavelength increased from 320 nm to 400 nm. It was also observed that the maximum PL emission intensity occurred at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm which was consistent with the test results in Fig. S6a. At the same time, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength showed an obvious bathochromic shift. When excitation wavelengths altered from 260 to 320 nm, their corresponding normalized emission maxima shifted from 419 to 470 nm (Fig. S6b) which indicated that the surface functionalization of CDs introduced trap states with different energy levels, leading to excitation-dependent emission [59,60]. The decay curves presented in Fig. 6c could be fitted with a double exponential function, and the lifetime of the CDs was calculated to be 6.95 ns. It was well known that the existence of surface states would lead to longer radiative lifetimes because carriers tended to be trapped by the surface domains [61,62]. The shorter lifetime τ1 could be attributed to the internal reorganization of the filled carbon core state, while the longer ones τ2 could be attributed to the surface-related luminescence. Under the optimal excitation wavelength (320 nm), the quantum yields (QYs) for CDs were calculated to be 10.6% according to the formula (2) by using quinine sulfate as the reference:

where, Φ = quantum yield; I = integrated fluorescence intensity; A = absorbance; η = refractive index; u = subscript to denote the sample, and s = subscript to denote the standard. After the introduction of CDs, the UV–vis absorption peaks of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT were located at 260 and 340 nm showing a slight red-shift compared with CDs (Fig. 6d). In addition, FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT series materials had strong absorption before 400 nm, indicating that they could well absorb the light in the UV band, and their UV absorption ability is stronger as the proportion of CDs in the overall material increased. It could be seen in the inset illustration of Fig. 6d that materials with different addition of CDs presented different color depths under 365 nm ultraviolet light. When the mass of CDs was 0.1 of OMMT, it emitted bright blue fluorescence, and the fluorescence became weaker as the ratio decrease to 0.01. Fig. 6e shows the optimal excitation peaks of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT located at 300–360 nm. Subsequently, we obtained the emission spectra of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT in different wavelengths (Fig. 6f-h). The positions of the emission peaks of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT series materials were slightly different under the same excitation wavelength and all had a redshift phenomenon, which shifted from 418 nm to 428 nm, 423–438 nm and 416–429 nm respectively. This can be explained by the rich surface defect states of CDs. When CDs are irradiated by the light of a specific wavelength, the photons that accumulate in the surface defect trap emit visible light of different wavelengths when they return to the ground state [63]. The high degree of oxidation on the surface of CDs creates abundant surface defects and emission spots, resulting in a red-shift of the emission wavelength. In addition, further functionalization of CDs by amine groups will also promote the occurrence of the red-shift phenomenon, which was attributed to the contribution of unpaired electrons from nitrogen to the conjugated graphene layers [57,64]. Moreover, the emission peak intensity was positively correlated with the addition of CDs. The time-resolved PL decay curve of each sample was also measured. The results (Fig. 6i) show a short-lived component τ1 and a long-lived component τ2 after being fitted by a dual-exponential formula. The average fluorescence lifetimes of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT, FSi-SiO2/0.02CDs@OMMT and FSi-SiO2/0.01CDs@OMMT were calculated to be 1.31 ns, 1.00 ns and 0.99 ns, respectively, which were weakened compared to that of CDs (6.95 ns).

Fig. 7a presents the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of MMT, 0.3FSi-SiO2 @MMT, 0.4FSi-SiO2 @MMT and 0.5FSi-SiO2 @MMT. The initial decomposition temperatures of MMT and the FSi-SiO2 @MMT series were about 350.2 ℃. The apparent water weight loss of them in the first stage (before 150 ℃) was about 3%. And the thermal weight loss rate of FSi-SiO2@MMT was significantly higher than that of MMT, which was due to the pyrolysis of organic matter and SiO2 nanoparticles connected to the surface of MMT and the organic fragments entering the interlayer of MMT [65,66]. As shown in the TGA curves of MMT, the weight loss of the -OH group of MMT was 7–8% within 250–550 °C, and the content of residual inorganic skeleton was up to 93.22% at 800 ℃. Separately, the TGA curves of FSi-SiO2@MMT series showed that the thermal degradation phase occurred at around 450–700 ℃. In addition, the thermal weight loss rates of the three composites in the second stage were 13.72%, 15.83% and 16.73% respectively, and the proportion of residual inorganic skeleton structure and undissolved organic matter at 800 ℃ were 80.38%, 77.72% and 76.46% respectively. The main reason for this phenomenon was that with the gradual increase of the addition of modifiers, the weight loss in the second thermal degradation stage would increase[67]. Comparing the curves of different FSi-SiO2@MMT composites, it showed little difference in the maximum decomposition temperature, which was o 538 ℃ for 0.3FSi-SiO2@MMT, 527 ℃ for 0.4 FSi-SiO2@MMT and 523 ℃ for 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT. Different from the dual-scale hierarchical structure materials above, in Fig. 7b, the apparent water loss at the first weight-loss stage (before 150 ℃) decreased to about 1.9%. And a large weight loss was observed at 250–440 °C, with 16.27%, 15.01%, 15.21%, and 16.14% respectively, which was caused by the thermal cleavage of the STAB between the layers of OMMT. Compared with OMMT, the thermal weight loss rate of FSi-SiO2/CDs@OMMT increases significantly in the range of 440–800 ℃ with weight loss of 4.02%, 4.62% and 14.49%, which was mainly due to the thermal decomposition of organic matter, CDs particles and SiO2 nanoparticles on the surface of OMMT at this stage [68]. In the end, 77.78%, 77.17% and 62.69% of inorganic skeletal structure and the undecomposed organic matter remained at 800 °C, and the thermal weight loss at this stage increased gradually with the addition of CDs.

The WCAs and WSAs of dual-scale hierarchical structure materials and multi-scale hierarchical structure materials were presented in Fig. 8 and Fig. 9. At first, MMT showed strong hydrophilicity, while it tended to be superhydrophobic after fluoroalkylsilane group and silica nanoparticles were grafted on the MMT surface (Fig. 8a-d). The superhydrophobic transformation of the FSi-SiO2@MMT could be achieved when the ratio reached 0.4 (WCA=152.67 ± 1°), which was attributed to the further addition of fluoroalkylsilane group and silica nanoparticles leading to a rougher structure of MMT layers and more abundant hydrophobic composition. On increasing the ratio further to 0.5, WCA reached 155.62 ± 1°. Correspondingly, the WSAs were reduced from 8.8 ± 0.2°to 4.8 ± 0.2° (Fig. 8e-g). OMMT showed a WCA of 125.05 ± 2°, meaning that wettability was obviously improved after being modified by STAB. Significantly, all samples that involved CDs presented a superhydrophobicity. In Fig. 9b, the WCA of FSi-SiO2/0.01CDs@OMMT was only 156.02 ± 1° which was similar to that of 0.5FSiSiO2@MMT (155.62 ± 1°). It might be due to the inability of the low content CDs particles to be uniformly distributed over a large area on the surface of large-scale micro-nano structure to form the requisite multi-scale hierarchical structure for further surface wettability enhancement. When the content of CDs nanoparticles increased further, the CDs were distributed evenly on the micro-nanostructure formed from MMT layers and SiO2 nanoparticles, which was in favor of building the necessary multi-scale hierarchical structure and made a distinct improvement in the WCAs (160.02 ± 2°, 169.22 ± 2°) (Fig. 9c, d). Accordingly, the WSAs of multi-scale hierarchical structure materials was significantly lower than that of dual-scale hierarchical structure materials and decreased with increasing CDs content (Fig. 9e-g). Based on the analysis above, we could conclude that it was the combination of the CDs nanoparticles, SiO2 nanoparticles and OMMT layers that resulted in a more compact multi-scale micro-nano structure than the dual-scale hierarchical structure and caused air to be easily trapped between the grooved structure of the film surface and water droplets, resulting in good super-hydrophobicity.

We tested the dispersion stability of different materials in water and styrene over 24 h. The results shown in Fig. 10a indicated that the MMT exhibited good hydrophilicity and swelling, which dispersed in water uniformly and stably. In contrast, the 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT floated on the water surface without settling showing super-hydrophobicity. It could be seen in Fig. 10b that both MMT and 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT appeared to settle in styrene solution at first, and the settling of MMT was more obvious than 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT. It was mainly due to the poor compatibility of MMT that was not modified by STAB with styrene. And it was found that 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT was still not compatible with styrene and gradually sank after standing for 0.5 h, 3 h and 24 h. Combined with the analysis of XRD data in Fig. 8b, this phenomenon was probably due to layer spacing of the MMT modified by FSi-SiO2 was not effectively improved. In Fig. 10c, since the hydrophobic capacity of OMMT was improved by STAB but still not superhydrophobic, and part of OMMT was dissolved in water and the other part floated on the water surface. FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT presented super-hydrophobicity floating on the water without settlement. In addition, compared to MMT, the compatibility of OMMT in styrene was good and did not change with time, which attributed to the grafting of STAB on the surface and interlayer of MMT, the long-chain alkyl of STAB improved the compatibility with the organic phase. As the same, FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT showed good dispersion stability in styrene at first, while a slight settlement occurred with the extension of time (Fig. 10d). It could be concluded that the dispersion stability of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT in styrene was significantly improved compared to 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT.

Good superhydrophobic stability and chemical resistance are essential for practical applications of super hydrophobic materials. We measured the chemical resistance of 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT under extreme chemical circumstances to further evaluate the superhydrophobic stability. As demonstrated in Fig. 11, 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT and FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT are placed in water solutions of different pH for 0, 24, 48, 72 h, and settling behavior at different times were recorded. Overall, FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT shows a better superhydrophobic stability than 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT. 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT showed a good acid resistance in acid solution and did not settle within 72 h, exhibiting good superhydrophobic stability. However, a slight settlement could be observed after 24 h when pH reached 7, and the more alkaline the solution, the more serious the settlement. Thus, the superhydrophobicity of 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT were destroyed in an alkaline environment. With regard to FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT, it is worth noting that there was no obvious settlement could be observed in any pH solutions within 72 h, even in strong alkaline solutions (pH 13). We speculated that this excellent superhydrophobic durability and acid-base resistance was related to the introduction of CDs. One of significant features of carbon materials was their strong tolerance to both acidic and alkaline solution [69–71]. Carbon dots distributed on the surface of the material acted as protective sites to resist the attack of acid and alkali. Another plausible explanation for this phenomenon was that the denser multiscale hierarchical micro/nano structure were formed after introducing CDs. Therefore, the amount of the trapped air in the micro-and nanospaces of the rough surface increased, which acted as a repelling agent for acid and alkali [72]. Increased WCAs of FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT also proved the formation of denser multiscale hierarchical micro/nano structure (Fig. 9d).

In this study, superhydrophobic materials with different scale hierarchical structures and blue fluorescence have been fabricated via a facile, low-cost, and scalable technique involving a biomimetic “lotus leaf effect”. 0.5FSi-SiO2@MMT had a dual-scale hierarchical structure that displayed superhydrophobicity, together with a water contact angle (WCA) of 155.62 ± 1° and a water sliding angle (WSA) of 4.8 ± 0.2°. Notably, FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT with a multi-scale hierarchical structure showed a better super-hydrophobicity (WCA，169.22 ± 2°; WSA，2.3 ± 0.2°) because of the denser micro-nano structure by introducing CDs. The combination of CDs imparted blue fluorescence properties to the superhydrophobic materials with an emission band at 430 nm. In addition, we found that surface modification of MMT increased the compatibility with the organic phase, which would make it more favorable for processing as a nano-filler for polymers. FSi-SiO2/0.1CDs@OMMT also possessed good heat and water resistance and dispersibility. And the chemical resistance was obviously improved by the introduction of CDs. The preparation method proposed in this work is simple, low-cost, and environmentally friendly, which will have mass production for practical applications in the future.
